caption: Fig. 1. EBSD (a–c) band contrast (BC) maps (d–f) phase maps overlaid by grain boundary maps and (g-i) KAM maps of the hot-rolled (a, d, g) HR45, (b, e, h) HR68, and (c, f, i) HR82 samples. The face-centered cubic (FCC) phase is shown in blue. RD and ND represent the rolling direction and normal direction, respectively. Black and red lines denote high-angle grain boundaries (HAGBs) with misorientation ≥ 15◦ and Σ3 (60◦〈111〉) grain boundaries, respectively. The KAM angle ranges from 0◦ to 5◦. The areas with high orientation gradients indicated the local strain concentration. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
paper_abstract: Special Steel Department, Central Iron and Steel Research Institute (CISRI), Beijing 100081, China
paper_title: Enhanced mechanical properties of a Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic low-density steel by increasing hot-rolling reduction
citation_sentences: The microstructures of the investigated samples at different rolling reductions are illustrated with EBSD maps, as shown in Fig. 1After hotrolling and subsequent water quenching, the microstructures of all the samples consisted of equiaxed FCC austenite grains with a large fraction of the first-order annealing twins (〈1 1 1〉 60◦ 3-grain boundaries) identified inside (denoted as red lines). The mean grain sizes of the austenite phase were measured as about 45.11, 27.02, and 18.80 μm for HR45, HR68, and HR82 samples, respectively. When the annealing twin boundaries were included, the mean effective grain sizes were notably reduced to about 21.82 μm, 11.55 μm, and 7.02 μm for HR45, HR68, and HR82 samples, respectively. The results showed that increasing hotrolling reduction gave rise to significant grain refinement and the annealing twin boundaries could effectively reduce the mean grain size of the austenite phase. The statistics of the 3 twin boundaries were measured to be 67%, 60%, and 52% (determined by length relative to the total length of the grain boundaries with misorientation angle >15◦) for HR45, HR68, and HR82 samples, respectively.

In order to show the dislocation distribution in the investigated samples, the Kernel average misorientation (KAM) values were calculated from the EBSD-raw data, and the KAM maps are shown in Fig. 1(gi). It is evident that the HR82 sample exhibited more areas with high orientation gradients than the other samples, suggesting that the local strain concentration due to the accumulation of dislocations was more prevalent with increasing hot-rolling reduction. The quantitative evaluation of the geometrically necessary dislocations (GND) densities in the present study was based on the approach following Kubin and Mortensen [18], who considered a GND array as simple cylinder torsion according to the strain gradient model of Gao et al. [19]. As defined, there was a series of twist boundaries in the cylinder, and each twist boundary consisted of two perpendicular arrays of screw dislocations, following which the relationship between GND density and misorientation angle θ was developed,

As shown in Fig. 1(a-c), the increase in hot-rolling reduction resulted in the refinement of the grain size. This was mainly attributed to the enhanced dynamic recrystallization (DRX) for the sample subjected to larger rolling reduction. Additionally, the intenser particle-stimulated nucleation (PSN) might be another influencing factor considering the existence of a large number of $( \mathrm { N b , M o , W ) C }$ particles (Fig. 2). Besides, the deformation-induced dislocation accumulation during hot-rolling led to the continuous increase in geometrically necessary dislocations (GND) density, as shown in Fig. 1(g-i). The parameters of the grain size and dislocation density are revealed in Fig. 8(a) for the understanding of the strengthening mechanism, $\mathrm { i . e . , }$ grain boundary strengthening and

In addition, it is interesting to note that the evolution of deformation substructures was faster in the samples at larger hot-rolling reductions, as displayed in Fig. 12. For instance, the slip band spacing at the engineering strain of 0.1 reduced with increasing hot-rolling reduction, which was measured to be 296.40 ± 50.06 nm, 155.02 ± 38.42 nm, and $6 8 . 0 5 \pm 8 . 9 1$ nm for the HR45, HR68, and HR82 samples, respectively (Fig. 11(a), (d), and (g)). In addition, microbands were observed at the engineering strain of 0.2 in the HR68 and HR82 samples (Fig. 11(b), (e), and (h)), which indicated a faster deformation substructure development compared to the HR45 sample. Essentially, this phenomenon was attributed to the different initial grain sizes of the samples at different hot-rolling reductions, which can be rationalized based on the dynamic slip band refinement model [20]. The glide plane softening effect leads to the dislocation multiplication on the same slip plane. The pile-up dislocations exert back stress on the dislocation sources and finally terminate the further emit of dislocations once the local stress acting on the dislocation sources falls below the critical activation stress. As shown in Fig. 1, the increase in hot-rolling reduction resulted in the finer grain size due to the enhanced DRX effect. Accordingly, the length of the slip band that generally passed through the whole grains decreased with decreasing grain size, indicating the distance between the pile-up dislocations in the slip band and the dislocation source was smaller. As a consequence, for the sample with finer grains, higher accumulated back stress acting on the dislocation source was obtained with the same amount of pile-up dislocations in the slip band. Thus, the dislocation sources will be terminated at a lower strain, suggesting the plastic strain accommodated by an individual slip band decreased with the finer grain size. The accommodation of the further plastic strain will hence require the activation of new dislocation sources, which leads to faster slip band refinement with decreasing grain size, and thus the long-range stress among slip bands was increased. This was the reason for the increased strain-hardening rates at stage II of the samples at a larger hot-rolling reduction. Nevertheless, the slip band spacing could not be further reduced once reached the limit of \~50 nm, as exhibited in Fig. 12, which suggested the saturation of the slip band refinement process. As a consequence, the faster slip band refinement contributed to the earlier saturation of this process, leading to the earlier generation of microbands and the loss of strain-hardening capability. Correspondingly, the duration of stage II decreased in the samples with finer grain sizes, which was the reason for the slight reduction of ductility.

citation_sentences: As revealed by SEM in Fig. 2, some second-phase precipitates existed in the microstructures of the investigated samples after hot rolling. The micron-sized polygonal ones were distributed along the rolling direction

The fracture surfaces of the investigated samples after the tensile test were examined using SEM, as exhibited in Fig. 6. The fractography of all the hot-rolled samples shows a typical ductile fracture with a combination of ductile dimples and various microvoids. In general, the (Nb, Mo,W)C particles located at the austenite grain boundaries (see Fig. 2) as a hard second phase particle will lead to the strain/stress concentration at their interfaces due to their deformation discrepancy with the austenite matrix. Thus, they tend to severed as the preferential nucleation sites for the microcracks. Nevertheless, the strain/stress concentration at the interfaces of these intergranular (Nb,Mo,W)C particles could be partially alleviated through the debonding of the intragranular (Nb,Mo,W)C particles and the subsequent formation of microvoids (see Fig. 6) [23,24]. Considering the abundantly observed ductile dimples on the fracture surface of the three investigated samples, the intergranular (Nb,Mo,W)C particles virtually played no detrimental effect on the toughness. With the increase in the rolling reduction, the ductile fracture morphology was retained, suggesting the toughness of the investigated samples was barely affected, which could also be verified by the Charpy impact test results (Fig. 5b). In addition, the size of the dimples in the HR45 sample was larger than the HR68 and HR82 samples, which was due to its larger mean grain size [25].

As shown in Fig. 1(a-c), the increase in hot-rolling reduction resulted in the refinement of the grain size. This was mainly attributed to the enhanced dynamic recrystallization (DRX) for the sample subjected to larger rolling reduction. Additionally, the intenser particle-stimulated nucleation (PSN) might be another influencing factor considering the existence of a large number of $( \mathrm { N b , M o , W ) C }$ particles (Fig. 2). Besides, the deformation-induced dislocation accumulation during hot-rolling led to the continuous increase in geometrically necessary dislocations (GND) density, as shown in Fig. 1(g-i). The parameters of the grain size and dislocation density are revealed in Fig. 8(a) for the understanding of the strengthening mechanism, $\mathrm { i . e . , }$ grain boundary strengthening and

dislocation strengthening. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 2 and Fig. 4 the presence of the (Nb,Mo,W)C particles and κ-carbides would give rise to the precipitate strengthening as well. Fig. 8(b) shows the volume fractions and mean particle sizes of the (Nb,Mo,W)C particles and κ-carbides of the three investigated samples. As listed in Table 1, with the increasing hot-rolling reduction from 45% to 82%, the ultimate tensile strength (UTS) and yield strength (YS) gradually increased from 861 ± 11 MPa and 480 ± 0 MPa to 976 ± 11 MPa and 648 ± 13 MPa, respectively. To reveal the strengthening factors that resulted in the increment of yield strength with increasing hot-rolling reduction, the contribution of the different strengthening mechanisms will be discussed and calculated.

citation_sentences: in the grain boundary and grain interior with sizes ranging from 1 to 25 μm. The submicron-sized spherical ones were distributed homogeneously in the austenite matrix. The mean particle sizes of the submicron-sized particles were 261 ± 36 nm, 256 ± 42 nm, and 259 ± 23 nm, and the volume fractions were \~ 0.193%, \~0.201%, and \~ 0.198%, respectively for the HR45, HR68, and HR82 samples. To further evaluate the chemical compositions and the crystal structures of these precipitates, EDS analysis was applied. The crystal structure of the precipitates was identified as the face-centered cubic (FCC) structure as shown in Fig. 3, and the lattice parameter was calculated as 4.36 Å.

citation_sentences: Fig. 4(a-c) shows the selected-area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns taken from the austenite matrix of the three investigated samples along the [1 0 0]-zone axis. The faint (1 0 0) and (1 0 1) superlattice reflections indicated the existence of the second phase with an ordered crystal structure. The corresponding TEM-dark field (DF) images taken from the same areas using the (1 0 0) superlattice reflection are shown in Fig. 4(d-f), demonstrating that a large number of nano-sized particles homogeneously precipitated inside the austenite grains during water quenching in all the samples. According to the similar observation reported in other Fe-Mn-Al-C alloys, these ordered finely dispersed particles were hence identified as the (Fe,Mn) AlC-type κ-carbides with an L′12-type structure, which was produced by the spinodal decomposition and subsequent ordering reaction during fast cooling without the further aging process [20–22]. The mean sizes of the κ-carbides were $0 . 8 1 \pm$ 0.26 nm, $0 . 7 8 \pm 0 . 1 7$ nm, and $0 . 8 3 \pm 0 . 2 3$ nm for the HR45, HR68, and HR82 samples, respectively. The weak diffraction spots indicated the low volume fractions of the κ-carbides, which were $3 . 5 \pm 0 . 3 3 \% , 3 . 3 \pm$ 0.52%, and $3 . 9 ~ \pm ~ 0 . 3 9 \%$ for the HR45, HR68, and HR82 samples, respectively.

dislocation strengthening. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 2 and Fig. 4 the presence of the (Nb,Mo,W)C particles and κ-carbides would give rise to the precipitate strengthening as well. Fig. 8(b) shows the volume fractions and mean particle sizes of the (Nb,Mo,W)C particles and κ-carbides of the three investigated samples. As listed in Table 1, with the increasing hot-rolling reduction from 45% to 82%, the ultimate tensile strength (UTS) and yield strength (YS) gradually increased from 861 ± 11 MPa and 480 ± 0 MPa to 976 ± 11 MPa and 648 ± 13 MPa, respectively. To reveal the strengthening factors that resulted in the increment of yield strength with increasing hot-rolling reduction, the contribution of the different strengthening mechanisms will be discussed and calculated.

citation_sentences: The room-temperature engineering stress (σe)-strain (εe) curves of the investigated samples at different hot-rolling reductions are illustrated in Fig. 5a, from which the mechanical properties are summarized in Table 1. The HR45 sample with a 45% rolling reduction exhibited an ultimate tensile strength (UTS) of 861 ± 11 MPa, a yield strength (YS) of $4 8 0 \pm 0 ~ \mathrm { M P a } ,$ and a total elongation of 60%. The increase in rolling reduction gave rise to the enhancement in both the UTS and YS and a minor loss of uniform elongation (UE) and total elongation (TE). With the increase in rolling reduction from 45% to 82%, the improvement of UTS and YS reached \~110 MPa, and \~ 170 MPa, respectively, while the UE decreased from 52.0 ± 1.0% to $^ { 3 9 . 0 \pm 0 . 8 , }$ and TE decreased from $6 0 \pm 1 \%$ to 50 ± 1%. In addition, all three samples possessed a similar reduction of area of >60%, which suggested their excellent ductility.

The impact toughness is the capability of energy absorption until fracture, which can be manifested by the Charpy V-notch (CVN) impact energy, as exhibited in Fig. 5b. All the samples at different hot-rolling reductions possessed outstanding Charpy impact energy above 100 J/ $\mathrm { { m } } ^ { 2 } .$ . The increase in hot-rolling reduction from 45% to 82% caused a minor loss of the Charpy impact energy from $1 3 3 \pm 2 . 5 \mathrm { ~ J / m } ^ { 2 }$ to 105 ± $1 . 2 ~ \mathrm { J / m ^ { 2 } }$ , suggesting good toughness was maintained.

The fracture surfaces of the investigated samples after the tensile test were examined using SEM, as exhibited in Fig. 6. The fractography of all the hot-rolled samples shows a typical ductile fracture with a combination of ductile dimples and various microvoids. In general, the (Nb, Mo,W)C particles located at the austenite grain boundaries (see Fig. 2) as a hard second phase particle will lead to the strain/stress concentration at their interfaces due to their deformation discrepancy with the austenite matrix. Thus, they tend to severed as the preferential nucleation sites for the microcracks. Nevertheless, the strain/stress concentration at the interfaces of these intergranular (Nb,Mo,W)C particles could be partially alleviated through the debonding of the intragranular (Nb,Mo,W)C particles and the subsequent formation of microvoids (see Fig. 6) [23,24]. Considering the abundantly observed ductile dimples on the fracture surface of the three investigated samples, the intergranular (Nb,Mo,W)C particles virtually played no detrimental effect on the toughness. With the increase in the rolling reduction, the ductile fracture morphology was retained, suggesting the toughness of the investigated samples was barely affected, which could also be verified by the Charpy impact test results (Fig. 5b). In addition, the size of the dimples in the HR45 sample was larger than the HR68 and HR82 samples, which was due to its larger mean grain size [25].

citation_sentences: As shown in Fig. 1(a-c), the increase in hot-rolling reduction resulted in the refinement of the grain size. This was mainly attributed to the enhanced dynamic recrystallization (DRX) for the sample subjected to larger rolling reduction. Additionally, the intenser particle-stimulated nucleation (PSN) might be another influencing factor considering the existence of a large number of $( \mathrm { N b , M o , W ) C }$ particles (Fig. 2). Besides, the deformation-induced dislocation accumulation during hot-rolling led to the continuous increase in geometrically necessary dislocations (GND) density, as shown in Fig. 1(g-i). The parameters of the grain size and dislocation density are revealed in Fig. 8(a) for the understanding of the strengthening mechanism, $\mathrm { i . e . , }$ grain boundary strengthening and

dislocation strengthening. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 2 and Fig. 4 the presence of the (Nb,Mo,W)C particles and κ-carbides would give rise to the precipitate strengthening as well. Fig. 8(b) shows the volume fractions and mean particle sizes of the (Nb,Mo,W)C particles and κ-carbides of the three investigated samples. As listed in Table 1, with the increasing hot-rolling reduction from 45% to 82%, the ultimate tensile strength (UTS) and yield strength (YS) gradually increased from 861 ± 11 MPa and 480 ± 0 MPa to 976 ± 11 MPa and 648 ± 13 MPa, respectively. To reveal the strengthening factors that resulted in the increment of yield strength with increasing hot-rolling reduction, the contribution of the different strengthening mechanisms will be discussed and calculated.

The volume fractions and mean particle sizes of the κ-carbides and (Nb,Mo,W)C particles of the three investigated samples are shown in Fig. 8(b). According to Eq.6, the strengthening contributions of the κ-carbides by the shearing mechanism were 60.16 MPa, 58.04 MPa, and 60.98 MPa for the HR45, HR68, and HR82 samples, respectively. Owing to their low volume fraction and the large size of the micron-sized (Nb, Mo,W)C particles, their strengthening contribution via Orowan bypassing mechanism was negligible. Based on Eq.7, the strengthening from the submicron-sized (Nb,Mo,W)C particles was calculated as 6.39 MPa, 6.65 MPa, and 6.52 MPa for the HR45, HR68, and HR82 samples, respectively. According to the calculated results above, it is reasonable to conclude that the precipitate strengthening did not contribute to the strength increment of the investigated samples with the larger hotrolling reduction.

The contribution of dislocation strengthening was attributed to the geometrically necessary dislocations (GNDs). The calculation results from the KAM maps (Fig. 8(a)) suggested that the mean GND density rose with the larger rolling reduction, since the unrelieved deformation energy after hot rolling was higher with the larger rolling reduction. The GND densities obtained from the KAM maps as already shown in Section 3.1 were $2 . 7 1 \times 1 0 ^ { 1 3 } \mathrm { m } ^ { - 2 } , 7 . 4 9 \times 1 0 ^ { 1 3 } \mathrm { m } ^ { - 2 }$ , and $9 . 8 7 \times 1 0 ^ { 1 3 } \mathrm { m } ^ { - 2 }$ for the HR45, HR68, and HR82 samples, respectively. The strengthening contribution of the dislocations was calculated by the Taylor hardening law [41]. Correspondingly, the strengthening arising from the dislocations was 56.29 MPa, 93.67 MPa, and 107.50 MPa for HR45, HR68, and HR82 samples, respectively.

citation_sentences: The grain boundary strengthening was ascribed to the barrier effect of grain boundaries to the dislocation movement during plastic deformation. In addition, the twin boundaries also could act as effective impedance sites to the motion of dislocations and reduce their mean free path, which plays a similar role as the HAGBs [42]. The effective grain size including annealing twin boundaries was applied in the present study when considering the grain boundary strengthening, which generally followed a Hall-Petch relationship. The dependence of the true flow stress at yielding $\sigma _ { F 0 }$ (obtained by excluding the contributions of precipitate strengthening $\sigma _ { p }$ and dislocation strengthening $\sigma _ { d s }$ from the yield strength $\sigma _ { y } )$ as a function of the inverse square root of the mean effective grain size $( d _ { e } ^ { - 1 / 2 } )$ was plotted in Fig. 9(a). According to the fitting result, the friction stress $\left( \sigma _ { 0 } \right)$ of 203.10 MPa and the Hall-Petch coefficient $( k _ { H P } )$ of 733.43 MPa μm1/2 were obtained from the y-axis intercept and slope, respectively. Hence, the contribution of grain boundary strengthening for HR45, HR68, and HR82 samples was calculated as 157.02 MPa, 215.84 MPa, and 276.78 MPa, respectively.

The contributions of the various strengthening mechanisms to the yield strength of the three investigated samples were shown in Fig. 9(b) and the calculated results were summarized in Table 2, showing good consistency with the experimental results and thus demonstrating the validity of the analysis of the strengthening mechanisms. As shown in Fig. 9(b), the grain boundary strengthening arising from both HAGBs and annealing twin boundaries was the predominant strengthening mechanism and the strengthening effect from the dislocations was also prominent. They were responsible for the increment in yield strength with increasing hot-rolling reduction.

paper_title: Enhanced mechanical properties of a Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic low-density steel by increasing hot-rolling reduction
citation_sentences: To reveal the mechanism contributing to the improved strengthductility combination of the present steel after hot rolling, the strainhardening rate (dσ/dε) vs. true strain (εt) curves of the investigated samples were exhibited in Fig. 10. The evolution trend of the strainhardening rate of the three investigated samples was similar, which exhibited an initial drop after yielding (stage I) followed by a gradual increase to their peak values (stage II) and then a continuous decrease until fracture (stage III), indicating a three-staged strain-hardening behavior. Despite the high SFE of ${ \sim } 7 3 \ \mathrm { m J / m } ^ { 2 } ,$ , the evolution of the strain-hardening rate was quite different with other conventional high-SFE fcc metals [43], which show a continuously decreasing trend with increasing strain. The three-staged strain-hardening behavior of the present steel led to the strain-hardening rate being maintained at a high level of \~2000 MPa over a wide range of plastic strain, contributing to the excellent ductility. In addition, the strain-hardening rates of the present steel increased continuously with increasing hot-rolling reduction.

The coplanar and non-coplanar slip traces of the {1 1 1} slip plane were denoted by the dashed lines. Microbands with ill-defined boundaries and distinct boundaries were denoted by the arrows and solid lines, respectively.
paper_abstract: Special Steel Department, Central Iron and Steel Research Institute (CISRI), Beijing 100081, China
paper_title: Enhanced mechanical properties of a Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic low-density steel by increasing hot-rolling reduction
citation_sentences: As widely accepted, the evolution of strain-hardening behavior is essentially governed by the development of deformation substructures. For the TRIP or TWIP steels, the similar “hump” of the stain-hardening curve, i.e., the increment stage of strain-hardening rate, was attributed to the activation of the secondary deformation mechanism [44,45]. In order to clarify the underlying mechanism resulting in the characteristic strain hardening behavior of the present steel, interrupted tensile tests at the engineering strain $\left( \varepsilon _ { e } \right)$ of 0.1, 0.2, and 0.4 were conducted and the corresponding deformation microstructures were characterized by TEM. The representative TEM-bright field (BF) images (taken along the 〈1 1 0〉 zone axis of the austenite matrix grains) are displayed in Fig. 11. The evolution pattern of the dislocation substructures of the samples at different rolling reductions was basically the same. At low strain, the arrays of planar slip bands were distributed homogeneously within the austenite matrix (Fig. 11(a, d, and g)). Moreover, the Taylor lattice formed by the intersections of two non-coplanar {1 1 1} slip traces was also observed, as indicated by the dashed lines in Fig. 11(a, d). With increasing plastic strain, the slip bands became more closely spaced (Fig. 11(b, e, and h)), and microbands were found with ill-defined boundaries and distinct boundaries marked with arrows and solid lines (Fig. 11(c, f, h, and i)), respectively. These dislocation substructures were the typical deformation pattern formed by predominant dislocation planar glide, which is generally observed in low-SFE materials, demonstrating the low three-dimensional mobility of the dislocations. However, the SFE of the present steel was calculated to be \~73 mJ/m2 . The high SFE of the investigated steel should facilitate the crossslip of dislocations and hence form the dislocation cell structure [46], which was against the planar glide dislocation substructures in the present steel. Gerold and Karnthaler [47] explained the dislocation planar glide behavior in the high-SFE material using the term “glide plane softening”, which is arising from the interaction between dislocations and κ-carbides [14,33,42,43]. Once the κ-carbide was sheared by the leading dislocation, a preferential glide path for the subsequent dislocations would be generated due to the reduced κ-carbide effective size [48], contributing to the formation of the planar slip bands and Taylor lattice. The further increase in strain led to the rotation of the Taylor lattice. As a result, microbands delineated by dislocation walls consisting of GNDs were generated to accommodate the orientation misfit between the Taylor lattice domains [49]. Therefore, the threestaged strain-hardening behavior of the present steel should be closely related to the transition of the planar deformation substructures, which will be discussed next.

At stage II, the strain-hardening rate increased continuously. The dislocation substructures at this stage of the three investigated samples were the fully-developed slip bands across the entire grains, as shown in Fig. 11(a), (b), (d), and (g). With increasing plastic strain, the spacing among the slip bands gradually reduced. The variation of the slip band spacing with increasing plastic strain of the three investigated samples was exhibited in Fig. 12. The continuous refinement of the slip bands was attributed to the activation of dislocation sources and the formation of new slip bands in the free spaces between the fully developed ones to accommodate the increasing plastic strain. As a result, the stored dislocation density increased continuously and homogeneously. Besides, the progressively reduced slip band spacings led to increasing longrange stress among slip bands [20]. Therefore, the strengthening effect from the above mechanisms led to the monotonously increased strainhardening rate at stage II. It is worth mentioning that the slip band spacing barely changed after reducing to \~50 nm, which indicated that the slip band refinement process during stage II was saturated and could not provide additional strain-hardening effect by reducing the slip band spacing. The accommodation of further plastic strain should be sustained by the formation of other dislocation substructures.

At stage III, the strain-hardening rate decreased slowly and remained at a high level (\~ 2000 MPa) until necking occurred. As observed in Fig. 11(c, e, f, h, and i), developing microbands with blurred boundaries and well-developed microbands with distinct boundaries were formed. This suggested that the further increase in plastic strain after the saturation of slip band refinement was accommodated by the formation and development of the microbands. The continuous development of the microbands also gradually increased the total dislocation density and subdivided the grains by their mutual intersections, i.e., the dynamic Hall-Petch effect, providing further strain-hardening effect [49]. Nevertheless, compared to the slip band refinement process which homogeneously increased the statistically stored (SSDs) dislocations and produced the long-range stress among the slip bands, the strainhardening effect from the formation of microbands was relatively weak since they consisted of GNDs [50]. Therefore, the strain-hardening rate gradually dropped during stage III.

In addition, it is interesting to note that the evolution of deformation substructures was faster in the samples at larger hot-rolling reductions, as displayed in Fig. 12. For instance, the slip band spacing at the engineering strain of 0.1 reduced with increasing hot-rolling reduction, which was measured to be 296.40 ± 50.06 nm, 155.02 ± 38.42 nm, and $6 8 . 0 5 \pm 8 . 9 1$ nm for the HR45, HR68, and HR82 samples, respectively (Fig. 11(a), (d), and (g)). In addition, microbands were observed at the engineering strain of 0.2 in the HR68 and HR82 samples (Fig. 11(b), (e), and (h)), which indicated a faster deformation substructure development compared to the HR45 sample. Essentially, this phenomenon was attributed to the different initial grain sizes of the samples at different hot-rolling reductions, which can be rationalized based on the dynamic slip band refinement model [20]. The glide plane softening effect leads to the dislocation multiplication on the same slip plane. The pile-up dislocations exert back stress on the dislocation sources and finally terminate the further emit of dislocations once the local stress acting on the dislocation sources falls below the critical activation stress. As shown in Fig. 1, the increase in hot-rolling reduction resulted in the finer grain size due to the enhanced DRX effect. Accordingly, the length of the slip band that generally passed through the whole grains decreased with decreasing grain size, indicating the distance between the pile-up dislocations in the slip band and the dislocation source was smaller. As a consequence, for the sample with finer grains, higher accumulated back stress acting on the dislocation source was obtained with the same amount of pile-up dislocations in the slip band. Thus, the dislocation sources will be terminated at a lower strain, suggesting the plastic strain accommodated by an individual slip band decreased with the finer grain size. The accommodation of the further plastic strain will hence require the activation of new dislocation sources, which leads to faster slip band refinement with decreasing grain size, and thus the long-range stress among slip bands was increased. This was the reason for the increased strain-hardening rates at stage II of the samples at a larger hot-rolling reduction. Nevertheless, the slip band spacing could not be further reduced once reached the limit of \~50 nm, as exhibited in Fig. 12, which suggested the saturation of the slip band refinement process. As a consequence, the faster slip band refinement contributed to the earlier saturation of this process, leading to the earlier generation of microbands and the loss of strain-hardening capability. Correspondingly, the duration of stage II decreased in the samples with finer grain sizes, which was the reason for the slight reduction of ductility.

caption: Fig. 12. The evolution of slip band spacing as a function of engineering strain of the investigated samples at different hot-rolling reductions.
paper_abstract: Special Steel Department, Central Iron and Steel Research Institute (CISRI), Beijing 100081, China
paper_title: Enhanced mechanical properties of a Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic low-density steel by increasing hot-rolling reduction
citation_sentences: At stage II, the strain-hardening rate increased continuously. The dislocation substructures at this stage of the three investigated samples were the fully-developed slip bands across the entire grains, as shown in Fig. 11(a), (b), (d), and (g). With increasing plastic strain, the spacing among the slip bands gradually reduced. The variation of the slip band spacing with increasing plastic strain of the three investigated samples was exhibited in Fig. 12. The continuous refinement of the slip bands was attributed to the activation of dislocation sources and the formation of new slip bands in the free spaces between the fully developed ones to accommodate the increasing plastic strain. As a result, the stored dislocation density increased continuously and homogeneously. Besides, the progressively reduced slip band spacings led to increasing longrange stress among slip bands [20]. Therefore, the strengthening effect from the above mechanisms led to the monotonously increased strainhardening rate at stage II. It is worth mentioning that the slip band spacing barely changed after reducing to \~50 nm, which indicated that the slip band refinement process during stage II was saturated and could not provide additional strain-hardening effect by reducing the slip band spacing. The accommodation of further plastic strain should be sustained by the formation of other dislocation substructures.

In addition, it is interesting to note that the evolution of deformation substructures was faster in the samples at larger hot-rolling reductions, as displayed in Fig. 12. For instance, the slip band spacing at the engineering strain of 0.1 reduced with increasing hot-rolling reduction, which was measured to be 296.40 ± 50.06 nm, 155.02 ± 38.42 nm, and $6 8 . 0 5 \pm 8 . 9 1$ nm for the HR45, HR68, and HR82 samples, respectively (Fig. 11(a), (d), and (g)). In addition, microbands were observed at the engineering strain of 0.2 in the HR68 and HR82 samples (Fig. 11(b), (e), and (h)), which indicated a faster deformation substructure development compared to the HR45 sample. Essentially, this phenomenon was attributed to the different initial grain sizes of the samples at different hot-rolling reductions, which can be rationalized based on the dynamic slip band refinement model [20]. The glide plane softening effect leads to the dislocation multiplication on the same slip plane. The pile-up dislocations exert back stress on the dislocation sources and finally terminate the further emit of dislocations once the local stress acting on the dislocation sources falls below the critical activation stress. As shown in Fig. 1, the increase in hot-rolling reduction resulted in the finer grain size due to the enhanced DRX effect. Accordingly, the length of the slip band that generally passed through the whole grains decreased with decreasing grain size, indicating the distance between the pile-up dislocations in the slip band and the dislocation source was smaller. As a consequence, for the sample with finer grains, higher accumulated back stress acting on the dislocation source was obtained with the same amount of pile-up dislocations in the slip band. Thus, the dislocation sources will be terminated at a lower strain, suggesting the plastic strain accommodated by an individual slip band decreased with the finer grain size. The accommodation of the further plastic strain will hence require the activation of new dislocation sources, which leads to faster slip band refinement with decreasing grain size, and thus the long-range stress among slip bands was increased. This was the reason for the increased strain-hardening rates at stage II of the samples at a larger hot-rolling reduction. Nevertheless, the slip band spacing could not be further reduced once reached the limit of \~50 nm, as exhibited in Fig. 12, which suggested the saturation of the slip band refinement process. As a consequence, the faster slip band refinement contributed to the earlier saturation of this process, leading to the earlier generation of microbands and the loss of strain-hardening capability. Correspondingly, the duration of stage II decreased in the samples with finer grain sizes, which was the reason for the slight reduction of ductility.
